title: Homoseh quahote

infobox.value: (meaning "orator of the stars" in [Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_language "Mohave language")) also called **Seck-a-hoot**, **Sicihoot** or **Sickahoot** in some English language sources; c. 1800 – c. 1872) was a hereditary leader of the [Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19661–5-1)

United States Office of Indian Affairs (1868). [*Annual report of the Commissioner of Indian Affairs, for the year 1867*](https://archive.org/stream/usindianaffairs67usdorich#page/160/mode/2up). Washington: G. P. O. "The conduct of the Hualapais the past year has been very reprehensible. In September a band of this tribe killed six Americans who were engaged in mining, at the time, near Fort Mohave. A party of citizens from the vicinity of the fort, accompanied by a number of Mohaves, led by a chief named Homoseh quahote, went in pursuit of the Hualapais, whom they overtook about 30 miles from the fort. Homoseh quahote, with his party, surprised the Hualapais in their camp, at daylight, and killed the whole party, 21 in number."

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Hereditary leader of the Mohave (c. 1800 – c. 1872)

| Homoseh quahote | |
| --- | --- |
| Homoseh quahote (Sicihoot), War Chief of the Mojaves, at Fort Mojave, Arizona, 1868 | |
|  | |
| [Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people") leader | |
| Preceded by | Unknown |
| Succeeded by | Empote quatacheech |
|  | |
| Personal details | |
| Born | Unknown  Arizona |
| Died | Unknown  [Fort Mohave](/wiki/Fort_Mohave "Fort Mohave") |
|  | |

**Homoseh quahote** (meaning "orator of the stars" in [Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_language "Mohave language")) also called **Seck-a-hoot**, **Sicihoot** or **Sickahoot** in some English language sources; c. 1800 – c. 1872) was a hereditary leader of the [Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19661–5-1)

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Homoseh_quahote&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Indians_at_Fort_Mojave%2C_Arizona%2C_Sicihoot%2C_War_Chief_of_the_Mojaves._%28Boston_Public_Library%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Indians_at_Fort_Mojave%2C_Arizona%2C_Sicihoot%2C_War_Chief_of_the_Mojaves._%28Boston_Public_Library%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indians_at_Fort_Mojave,_Arizona,_Sicihoot,_War_Chief_of_the_Mojaves._(Boston_Public_Library)_(cropped).jpg)

Homoseh quahote stands in front of a group of Indians at Fort Mojave, Arizona, 1868.

Homoseh quahote was a member of the *Malika* clan of [Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people") and designated as *Aha macave pipataho*, which tribal elder Gwegwi nuor of the *Oach* clan translated as the leader "looked up to by the people because of the kind of person he was".[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19662-2) In 1966, Gwegwi nuor provided the only known description of him:

> Chief Humsoquahoat is over 6 ft tall and not heavy built but the scars on his breast shows that he has fought many people for justice or carry out the set up [of] our government. His hair is long, he's painted in red, white and black and armed and [a] few feathers on his head.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19664-3)

In 1861, Homoseh quahote abdicated the position as leader of the Mohave to [Irataba](/wiki/Irataba "Irataba"), who served in that capacity until at least 1866 or 1867, though opinions differ. By 1870, Seck-a-hoot had regained his position.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer19661–12-4) The exact year of his death is unknown, but the last official correspondence from the [Fort Mohave](/wiki/Fort_Mohave "Fort Mohave") Indian Reservation that mention him are dated to 1872.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer196610–4-5) He was succeeded as leader of the Mohave by his son, *Empote quotacheech*.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherer196613-6)

In 1867, Homoseh quahote was reported to have been part of a group that killed an entire party of 21 [Hualapais](/wiki/Hualapai "Hualapai") after they had murdered a group of six American miners in the Fort Mohave area.[[7]](#cite_note-USOIA-7)
